title: How Well Do You Interpret the Bible?
description: Ten questions about context, genre, original audience, biblical theology and faithful application.

questions.question: What is usually the best first step when interpreting a Bible verse?
options: Ask what the verse means to you personally
Read the sentences and argument around it
Search for another verse with the same word
Decide whether you agree with it
questions.explanation: A verse belongs within a paragraph, chapter and book. Reading the surrounding passage helps us follow the author’s argument and prevents isolated proof-texting.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: Why is the original audience important?
options: The Bible applied only to its first readers
Knowing their situation helps explain what the author was addressing
Modern readers must reproduce every ancient custom
The original audience determines whether a book is inspired
questions.explanation: Biblical authors wrote to real people facing particular questions, pressures and cultural circumstances. Understanding that setting helps us distinguish the original message from its faithful application today.

questions.question: Which statement best explains the difference between descriptive and prescriptive passages?
options: Descriptive passages are true while prescriptive passages are symbolic
Descriptive passages report what happened while prescriptive passages instruct what should happen
Descriptive passages occur only in the Old Testament
Prescriptive passages must always be applied without considering context
questions.explanation: A narrative may accurately describe behaviour without approving or commanding it. Readers must ask whether Scripture is reporting an event, evaluating it or presenting it as a pattern to follow.

questions.question: How should biblical poetry normally be read?
options: As straightforward scientific description
As imagery, parallelism and emotionally expressive language
As a code in which every object predicts a future event
As less truthful than biblical narrative
questions.explanation: Poetry communicates truth through imagery, metaphor, repetition and parallel lines. Recognising these features prevents figurative language from being forced into literal scientific claims.

questions.question: What is a common danger in biblical word studies?
options: Checking how a word is used elsewhere
Assuming a word carries every possible dictionary meaning each time it appears
Comparing several translations
Considering the sentence in which the word occurs
questions.explanation: Words take their particular meaning from context. Importing every possible definition or a word’s full history into one occurrence can produce meanings the author never intended.

questions.question: When interpreting an Old Testament law, which question is most helpful?
options: How can it be enforced directly in every modern nation?
What role did it play in Israel’s covenant and how is it related to Christ?
Can it be ignored because it is old?
Does it match current social expectations?
questions.explanation: Old Testament laws belonged to Israel’s covenant life. Christians should consider their purpose, the kind of law involved and how its moral wisdom is fulfilled and reframed through Christ.
questions.link_text: Read How Should Christians Read Old Testament Law?

questions.question: Which approach is most helpful when reading a parable of Jesus?
options: Assign a secret meaning to every minor detail
Identify the main challenge or kingdom message in its setting
Treat it as a literal report of an historical event
Assume every parable teaches exactly the same lesson
questions.explanation: Parables use memorable stories and comparisons to reveal the kingdom, confront assumptions and call hearers to respond. Details support the central point but should not automatically become an elaborate hidden code.

questions.question: What should readers expect when approaching apocalyptic literature such as parts of Daniel or Revelation?
options: A plain chronological newspaper-style account
Symbolic visions drawing on earlier Scripture and the struggles of God’s people
Predictions that can be understood without knowing the Old Testament
A message intended only for the final generation
questions.explanation: Apocalyptic literature communicates through vivid symbols, visions and echoes of earlier Scripture. Its first hearers were encouraged to remain faithful under pressure, even as it points towards God’s final victory.
questions.link_text: See the site’s current end-times position

questions.question: What does it mean to read Scripture canonically?
options: Read only the books everyone agrees belong in the canon
Interpret each passage within its book and the wider biblical story
Give later books permission to cancel earlier Scripture
Arrange verses according to theological topics
questions.explanation: A canonical reading respects each passage in its own setting while also considering its place within the complete scriptural story of creation, covenant, Christ, church and new creation.
questions.link_text: Read How We Got the Bible

questions.question: Which statement best describes faithful application?
options: Repeating the original action exactly in every culture
Moving from the passage’s original meaning to a fitting response today
Beginning with a modern issue and finding a verse that supports your view
Applying only passages that feel personally relevant
questions.explanation: Faithful application begins with what the text communicated in its original context, identifies the enduring theological or moral principle and considers how obedience should take shape in the present setting.

questions.question: What does literary context refer to?
options: The political views of the modern reader
The verses, paragraphs and argument surrounding a passage
The history of every English translation
The personal experience of the preacher
questions.explanation: Literary context includes the sentences, paragraphs, sections and whole book surrounding a passage. It helps determine how the author’s thought develops.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: What is proof-texting?
options: Checking whether a quotation is accurate
Using an isolated verse to support a claim without respecting its context
Comparing several related passages
Studying the manuscript evidence for a reading
questions.explanation: Proof-texting detaches a verse from its literary and historical setting and uses it to support a conclusion the passage may not actually teach.

questions.question: Why should readers identify a passage’s genre?
options: Genre determines whether the passage is inspired
Different kinds of writing communicate in different ways
Only poetry requires interpretation
Genre tells readers which passages may be ignored
questions.explanation: Narrative, poetry, law, prophecy, wisdom, letters and apocalyptic visions use different conventions. Genre helps readers ask the right questions of the text.

questions.question: Which approach is most responsible when a proverb appears to have exceptions?
options: Reject the proverb as false
Read it as a general observation about wise living rather than an unconditional promise
Assume every exception proves a lack of faith
Treat it as a legal command
questions.explanation: Biblical proverbs normally express general patterns of wisdom. They are not always absolute guarantees about what will happen in every circumstance.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of consulting the historical setting of a passage?
options: To replace the biblical text with archaeology
To understand the circumstances, customs and issues addressed by the author
To prove modern culture is superior
To make every passage apply only to the ancient world
questions.explanation: Historical background can clarify customs, conflicts and assumptions known to the first audience. It should illuminate the text rather than replace it.

questions.question: When a New Testament letter says 'you', what should readers remember?
options: It is always addressed to one modern individual
It may address a whole congregation rather than one person
It never applies beyond the first century
It must be translated as a command
questions.explanation: Many New Testament letters were written to churches. The plural form of 'you' can shape how commands and promises are understood communally.

questions.question: What is the danger of reading modern meanings back into an ancient word?
options: It makes the passage too easy
The word may not have carried that modern meaning in the author’s setting
Ancient words never change meaning
Modern dictionaries are always more accurate than context
questions.explanation: Words change over time. Responsible interpretation asks how a word was used in its sentence, book and historical setting rather than assuming its modern meaning.

questions.question: What should guide the interpretation of a biblical symbol?
options: Whatever association first comes to mind
How the symbol functions in its passage and elsewhere in Scripture
A fixed modern chart
The numerical value of every letter
questions.explanation: Biblical symbols should be interpreted from their immediate context, historical background and scriptural usage rather than from imaginative associations alone.

questions.question: How should readers approach apparent tensions between Bible passages?
options: Ignore whichever passage is less familiar
Study each passage in context before deciding whether a real contradiction exists
Assume one author must be mistaken
Combine the wording immediately without respecting either author
questions.explanation: Passages may address different settings, questions or aspects of a subject. Careful comparison begins by understanding each text on its own terms.

questions.question: What is biblical theology?
options: Arranging every doctrine alphabetically
Tracing themes and God’s unfolding work across the biblical story
Studying only the books written before Jesus
Replacing exegesis with church tradition
questions.explanation: Biblical theology traces themes such as creation, covenant, kingdom, temple, exile and new creation through Scripture’s unfolding story.
questions.link_text: Read about the wider biblical story

questions.question: What is exegesis?
options: Drawing meaning out of the text through careful study
Reading a preferred idea into the text
Memorising a passage without interpretation
Translating only from English
questions.explanation: Exegesis seeks to draw out the meaning communicated by the text. Its opposite, eisegesis, reads ideas into the passage.

questions.question: Which question is most useful when reading a New Testament letter?
options: What situation or problem prompted the author to write?
Which modern political party would the author support?
How can every sentence be turned into a promise?
Which verses can be separated from the argument?
questions.explanation: Letters address particular communities and concerns. Reconstructing the issue being addressed helps readers follow the author’s reasoning.

questions.question: What does it mean that the Bible was written for us but not directly to us?
options: The Bible has no present authority
Its message matters today, but its books first addressed ancient audiences
Only scholars can understand Scripture
Modern readers should imitate every cultural detail
questions.explanation: Scripture is for the church across time, yet each book was written to particular people. Application begins by respecting that original communication.
questions.link_text: Read about written to and written for

questions.question: How should narrative characters normally be treated?
options: Everything they do should be copied
Their actions should be evaluated through the narrator and wider Scripture
Every character represents a hidden doctrine
Only their spoken words matter
questions.explanation: Biblical narratives often describe flawed people. The story’s evaluation, consequences and wider scriptural teaching help determine whether conduct is exemplary.

questions.question: What is a sound way to use a study Bible or commentary?
options: Read it before examining the passage yourself
Use it after initial observation to test and deepen your understanding
Treat its notes as inspired Scripture
Choose only resources that confirm your first opinion
questions.explanation: Helpful resources can supply background and alternative readings, but they should support rather than replace careful engagement with the biblical text.

questions.question: What should readers do when responsible interpreters disagree?
options: Assume disagreement proves the Bible is meaningless
Compare the evidence, remain humble and distinguish central claims from uncertain details
Select the view held by the most popular speaker
Avoid reaching any conclusion
questions.explanation: Interpretive humility recognises that faithful readers can disagree. Evidence, context and theological importance should shape how confidently a conclusion is held.
questions.link_text: Read about the Humble Theologian approach

questions.question: Which statement best describes typology?
options: Finding arbitrary secret codes in every detail
Recognising patterns in earlier Scripture that reach fuller expression in Christ and the biblical story
Treating the Old Testament as irrelevant
Replacing historical events with allegory
questions.explanation: Typology recognises divinely shaped patterns such as Adam, exodus, sacrifice, temple and kingship that develop across Scripture and find fuller expression in Christ.
questions.link_text: Read about the whole biblical story

questions.question: How should a modern application relate to the passage’s original meaning?
options: It may contradict the original meaning if it is helpful
It should grow from the passage’s meaning and enduring theological principle
It should repeat the ancient practice without adjustment
It should depend only on personal feelings
questions.explanation: Application may take a different cultural form, but it should remain faithful to the truth, purpose and moral direction of the passage.

questions.question: Why is repeated reading useful in interpretation?
options: It removes the need for context
It helps readers notice structure, repeated words, contrasts and connections
It guarantees agreement with every commentator
It turns every passage into poetry
questions.explanation: Repeated reading slows us down and reveals features that are easily missed, including structure, emphasis, transitions and recurring themes.

questions.question: What is the best posture for interpreting Scripture?
options: Confidence that personal assumptions are always correct
Prayerful attention, humility, careful study and willingness to be corrected
Suspicion of every translation
Dependence on isolated impressions
questions.explanation: Faithful interpretation involves both disciplined study and a humble readiness to hear and obey God’s word within the community of faith.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible
